refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire volumetric or mass flow data from material handling subsystems during operation.
Calculate throughput rate (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired flow data.
Display or transmit throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
Log historical throughput data for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Periodically sample material quantity at fixed time intervals, compute differential changes, and extrapolate to period-level throughput estimate
DP3: Instrument material input and output boundaries with independent accumulation counters (mass or volume), compute net throughput as difference, and normalize by elapsed time
DP4: Decompose throughput into constituent process stages (e.g., feed rate, processing rate, discharge rate), measure each independently, and synthesize via process model to infer system throughput
DP5: Establish reference material batches of known mass/volume, correlate batch cycle time to throughput, and report productivity as batch count per unit time
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Instrument material input and output boundaries with independent accumulation counters (mass or volume), compute net throughput as difference, and normalize by elapsed time

potential_sources: Boundary definition coupling: Input and output measurement points must be spatially and temporally well-defined to avoid ambiguity in what constitutes 'system throughput' vs. intermediate losses or hold-up
Counter synchronization: If input and output counters operate asynchronously, clock drift or timing misalignment could introduce systematic bias in net throughput calculation

Material state assumption: DP3 assumes material properties (density, compressibility) remain stable during evaluation; if material composition or moisture content varies significantly, mass-based counters may not correlate linearly to volumetric throughput
Weak coupling to DP1 and DP2: DP3 is less sensitive to instantaneous flow dynamics than continuous sensing (DP1), reducing real-time feedback capability but improving robustness to transient noise
coupling_check.notes: DP3 exhibits low coupling to external process dynamics because it measures net accumulation rather than instantaneous flow. This makes it resilient to measurement noise and process variability. However, it introduces a latency: throughput is only known after the evaluation period closes. Coupling to worksite safety is minimal (counters are passive). Coupling to operational policies depends on whether batch-level or continuous reporting is mandated.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material does not accumulate or deplete within the system boundaries during the evaluation period (steady-state assumption). Risk: If material hold-up changes significantly, net throughput will be biased. Mitigation: Measure and account for inventory delta.

Assumption: Input and output measurement points are accessible and can be instrumented without disrupting operations. Risk: Worksite constraints may prevent ideal placement. Mitigation: Conduct site survey early; consider non-invasive sensing alternatives if access is limited.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target' is evaluated over a single contiguous evaluation period. Risk: If evaluation period is short relative to process cycle time, statistical confidence is low. Mitigation: Define minimum evaluation duration (e.g., ≥ 10 full cycles or ≥ 8 hours).

Assumption: Counter accuracy and calibration are traceable to a standard. Risk: Uncalibrated or drifting counters will systematically bias reported throughput. Mitigation: Establish pre- and post-evaluation calibration protocol.

Assumption: Material properties (density for mass-to-volume conversion, if needed) are known or can be measured independently. Risk: Heterogeneous material or moisture variation introduces conversion error. Mitigation: Specify material specification or measure density during evaluation.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan does not prohibit instrumentation of input/output boundaries. Risk: Safety constraints may force alternative DP selection. Mitigation: Engage safety officer early in design phase.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): Enable measurement and reporting of net material throughput (mass or volume per unit time) at system input and output boundaries, with reported productivity validated against productivity_target over a defined evaluation period, subject to worksite safety and operational policy constraints. Clarify: (1) Is real-time feedback required, or is post-period reporting acceptable? (2) What is the minimum evaluation duration and acceptable statistical confidence interval? (3) Are input and output boundaries physically accessible for instrumentation? (4) Is material composition stable, or must density/volume conversion be dynamic? Answering these will refine DP selection and reduce risk of IM mismatch.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized material flow monitoring and throughput computation subsystem that instruments input/output boundaries, maintains independent accumulation counters, and derives net throughput metrics normalized by elapsed time.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit computed net throughput, accumulation counter states, and material flow metrics to external monitoring/logging systems

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive configuration parameters for counter initialization, boundary definitions, and throughput computation intervals

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts if throughput deviates beyond safe operating thresholds or counter anomalies are detected

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: material flow instrumentation, accumulation logic, and throughput computation are core to NGHE operational monitoring. Counter state management and time-normalized calculations are self-contained. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces require external coupling, but these are standard observability/control channels.

Poor containment: Vehicle module typically owns propulsion, navigation, and structural systems. Material flow instrumentation is a monitoring/analytics function orthogonal to vehicle dynamics. High diffusion: would require Vehicle to host telemetry aggregation, counter logic, and time-series computation—violating separation of concerns.

Poor containment: Teleoperation owns remote command/control and operator interface logic. Material throughput computation is a backend analytics function, not a teleoperation concern. High diffusion: would scatter counter state and throughput logic across operator-facing and backend systems.

Moderate-to-poor containment: Safety Services owns hazard detection and alert generation. While throughput anomalies may trigger safety responses, the core accumulation and normalization logic is not inherently a safety function—it is a measurement/analytics function. Moderate diffusion: Safety Services would need to embed material flow instrumentation, which is a domain mismatch.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP (independent accumulation counters, net throughput computation, time normalization) is a material flow analytics and monitoring function that belongs in the NGHE Solution module. NGHE Solution is the natural owner of operational metrics, throughput tracking, and system-level observability. The DP does not belong in Vehicle (propulsion/navigation), Teleoperation (remote control), or Safety Services (hazard response); those modules would suffer cohesion loss and coupling increase if forced to host this logic.
containment_risks: If material input/output sensors are physically distributed across Vehicle or other subsystems, sensor data acquisition may require Vehicle-level integration; mitigate by defining a clean sensor data interface (e.g., via Telemetry or a dedicated sensor abstraction layer).

If throughput thresholds trigger safety-critical responses, SafetyAlert coupling may become bidirectional; mitigate by keeping Safety Services as a consumer of throughput alerts, not a co-owner of counter logic.

If multiple independent NGHE instances exist (e.g., in a fleet), counter state synchronization and distributed time normalization may introduce complexity; mitigate by documenting clock synchronization assumptions and counter reconciliation protocols.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. The DP specifies a complete logical solution: independent accumulation counters at input/output boundaries, net throughput computation as difference, and time normalization. This maps cleanly to the functional requirement to enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time. No logical gaps or ambiguities detected.

diffusion_risks: Sensor data acquisition: If material input/output sensors are physically distributed across Vehicle or other subsystems, sensor data integration may create a hidden dependency. Mitigation: Define a clean sensor abstraction interface (e.g., standardized sensor data contract) that decouples NGHE Solution from sensor hardware location.

SafetyAlert bidirectionality: If throughput anomalies trigger safety-critical responses that feed back into counter logic or recalibration, Safety Services coupling may become bidirectional and create circular dependencies. Mitigation: Enforce unidirectional flow—NGHE Solution emits alerts; Safety Services consumes and acts independently.

Distributed synchronization: If multiple NGHE instances operate in a fleet, clock skew and counter state reconciliation across instances may introduce subtle timing and consistency issues. Mitigation: Document clock synchronization assumptions (e.g., NTP tolerance, counter reconciliation protocol) and validate in integration testing.

Telemetry coupling: While Telemetry is a standard observability channel, high-frequency throughput reporting may create latency or bandwidth constraints. Mitigation: Define telemetry sampling rates and buffering policies explicitly in the interface contract.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score—excellent. The core DP logic (accumulation counters, net throughput computation, time normalization) is self-contained within NGHE Solution's operational monitoring scope. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are standard observability and control channels, not diffusion of core logic. Alternative candidates (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) all exhibit poor-to-moderate containment (0.28–0.45) and high diffusion (0.55–0.72) due to domain mismatch: Vehicle owns propulsion/navigation, Teleoperation owns remote control, Safety Services owns hazard detection—none are natural owners of material flow analytics. NGHE Solution is the correct architectural home.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over the agreed evaluation period' is directly measurable via NGHE Solution's throughput computation output. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of input/output boundaries with calibrated sensors, (2) validation of counter accumulation logic against ground truth (e.g., manual material audits), (3) time normalization accuracy check (clock synchronization validation), and (4) end-to-end throughput reporting against productivity_target. All are testable within representative operations.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is unambiguously allocated ownership of material flow instrumentation, accumulation counter state, net throughput computation, and time normalization. Interfaces to Telemetry (output), Tasking (input), and SafetyAlert (output) are well-defined. Sensor data acquisition responsibility should be explicitly clarified: if sensors are Vehicle-hosted, define a sensor data interface contract; if NGHE-hosted, ensure physical integration is documented. Worksite safety plan and operational policies should specify throughput thresholds and alert escalation paths to Safety Services.

recommended_revisions: Add explicit sensor data interface specification: Define the contract for input/output boundary sensor data (e.g., sensor type, sampling rate, data format, latency tolerance) to decouple NGHE Solution from sensor hardware location and reduce Vehicle-level coupling.

Document clock synchronization and counter reconciliation protocols: For single or multi-instance deployments, specify NTP tolerance, counter state reconciliation logic, and handling of clock skew to mitigate distributed synchronization risks.

Clarify SafetyAlert unidirectionality: Explicitly state that SafetyAlert is a one-way emission from NGHE Solution to Safety Services; Safety Services shall not feed back control signals that modify counter logic or throughput computation.
Define telemetry sampling and buffering policy: Specify throughput reporting frequency, buffering strategy, and latency bounds to prevent telemetry coupling from becoming a performance bottleneck.

Establish productivity_target validation procedure: Document how productivity_target is derived, how it maps to throughput units (mass/volume per unit time), and the evaluation period duration to ensure acceptance criteria are unambiguous and testable.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and containment analysis strongly validates NGHE Solution as the correct architectural owner. However, the gate results identify five concrete, actionable revisions that must be incorporated before proceeding to sub-level decomposition. These revisions address critical architectural clarity gaps—particularly sensor data abstraction, clock synchronization, SafetyAlert unidirectionality, and telemetry coupling—that, if left unresolved, will propagate ambiguity and diffusion risk into lower-level FRs. Given the high safety criticality of this system, resolving these at the current level is essential to establish a clean, verifiable foundation for decomposition. Proceeding to ZIGZAG_DOWN without revision would lock in these ambiguities and force rework downstream.

revise.why: The DP is logically sound but architecturally underspecified. The five recommended revisions must be integrated into the DP specification to clarify: (1) sensor data interface contract (decoupling NGHE Solution from sensor hardware location), (2) clock synchronization and counter reconciliation protocols (mitigating distributed synchronization risk), (3) SafetyAlert unidirectionality constraint (preventing circular dependencies with Safety Services), (4) telemetry sampling and buffering policy (preventing observability coupling from becoming a bottleneck), and (5) productivity_target validation procedure (ensuring acceptance criteria are unambiguous and testable). These are not new functional requirements; they are architectural constraints and interface specifications that must be explicit in the DP to enable clean decomposition and verification.

assumptions: Sensor data acquisition responsibility is either Vehicle-hosted (with a clean sensor data interface contract) or NGHE-hosted (with documented physical integration). This must be clarified in the revised DP.
Clock synchronization (e.g., NTP) is available and tolerances are acceptable for counter state consistency. Tolerance bounds must be documented in the revised DP.
SafetyAlert is unidirectional: NGHE Solution emits alerts; Safety Services consumes and acts independently without feeding back control signals that modify counter logic.

Telemetry infrastructure can support the throughput reporting frequency required by the acceptance criterion without introducing latency or bandwidth bottlenecks. Sampling rates and buffering policies must be specified in the revised DP.

Productivity_target is derived from operational requirements and is expressible in the same units as the throughput computation (mass/volume per unit time). Derivation and validation procedure must be documented in the revised DP.
open_risks: Sensor data integration: If input/output sensors are distributed across Vehicle or other subsystems, integration complexity and latency may exceed acceptable bounds. Mitigation: Define sensor abstraction interface in revised DP; validate in integration testing.

SafetyAlert bidirectionality: If Safety Services feeds back control signals that modify counter logic or recalibration, circular dependencies and race conditions may emerge. Mitigation: Enforce unidirectionality in revised DP; document in interface contract.

Distributed synchronization: In multi-instance deployments, clock skew and counter state reconciliation may introduce subtle timing and consistency issues. Mitigation: Document clock synchronization assumptions and counter reconciliation protocol in revised DP; validate in fleet testing.

Telemetry coupling: High-frequency throughput reporting may create latency or bandwidth constraints. Mitigation: Define telemetry sampling rates and buffering policies in revised DP; monitor in operational deployment.

Productivity_target ambiguity: If productivity_target is not clearly derived and validated, acceptance criteria may be disputed. Mitigation: Document derivation, unit mapping, and evaluation period in revised DP.
evidence_needed: Sensor data interface specification: Define contract for input/output boundary sensor data (type, sampling rate, data format, latency tolerance, error handling).
Clock synchronization and counter reconciliation protocol: Specify NTP tolerance, counter state reconciliation logic, handling of clock skew, and validation procedure.
SafetyAlert interface specification: Explicitly document unidirectionality, alert trigger conditions, alert payload format, and expected Safety Services response (without feedback to counter logic).
Telemetry sampling and buffering policy: Specify throughput reporting frequency, buffering strategy, latency bounds, and bandwidth requirements.
Productivity_target validation procedure: Document derivation methodology, unit mapping (mass/volume per unit time), evaluation period duration, and acceptance threshold.

Integration test plan: Validate sensor data acquisition, clock synchronization, SafetyAlert emission, telemetry delivery, and end-to-end throughput reporting against productivity_target in representative operational scenarios.
